feedback.text: Video games have taken an important part in my development as an adult person throughout the years. Either by being a form of simple entertainment, brain enriching puzzles or artistic commentaries on real life, they have contributed to defining my persona, helping me find meaningful ways of spending time with friends, playing with my brain to find efficient solutions to a diverse set of problems, and exposing me to concepts and ideas that challenged the conservative beliefs that were forced on me. I consider each game that has ever been crafted as a work of art, one that each generation of the future could experience, even if the original piece was creator hundreds or thousands of years earlier. Even if the message conveyed is a bad one in cases of story games, it shouldn't be silenced, but rather displayed as a bad case and as an insight into the way the people who made the game think. What we are witnessing right now on an international scale is the destruction and silencing of these pieces of art. Game companies don't care about making games accessible for the future, insisting on online-required features that will cripple a lot of games upon the inevitable closure of said game's servers. I would go as far as accusing said companies of intentionally implementing mandatory online features so that they can kill their game in the future when they release a sequel, so current playerbases will have to buy the new game if they wish to continue playing.
